exemplar.title: To what extent can Geometric Brownian Motion accurately predict the stock prices of Apple?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent can Geometric Brownian Motion accurately predict the stock prices of Apple?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated as an evaluative inquiry and effectively guides the investigation.
The topic and purpose (testing GBM on Appleâs stock over a defined horizon) are communicated accurately and maintained throughout.
The methodology outlines relevant data collection, parameter estimation and Monte-Carlo simulation steps with reasonable clarity.
weaknesses: Background sections contain tangential or repetitive material that does not advance the mathematical focus.
Key methodological assumptions (constant volatility) are acknowledged but no sensitivity analyses or alternative model comparisons are performed.
The choice of a 30-day forecast horizon is not justified or linked to model validity criteria.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates the topic and research question clearly (band 3â4), with a mostly coherent methodology that covers appropriate data sources and simulation steps. Occasional digressions and lack of robustness checks keep this criterion solidly in band 3â4, warranting 4 of 6 marks.

strengths: Solid application of stochastic calculus in deriving the discrete GBM formula and applying ItÃ´âs lemma.
Key parameters (drift, volatility) are defined and linked to the simulation context.
Terminology such as Wiener process, MSE, RMSE and MAPE is used accurately and consistently in most cases.
weaknesses: An implausibly high annualized drift reveals a scaling error that undermines conceptual rigour.
Use of non-standard terms (e.g. âdecreasementsâ) and occasional language slips reduce precision.
The literature review is narrow and includes questionable academic sources (AI chat prompts).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates good subject knowledge and generally accurate use of mathematical terminology (band 3â4), but conceptual lapses (drift scaling) and occasional imprecision prevent it reaching excellence. A score of 4 of 6 reflects this solid but not outstanding performance.

strengths: Analysis includes derivation of the GBM solution, parameter estimation and 1 000-path Monte-Carlo simulation with multiple error metrics.
Discussion clearly links results to the research question, noting both model strengths (trend tracking, low MAPE) and weaknesses (volatility underestimation).
The student proposes GARCH as an alternative, demonstrating critical consideration of model limitations.
weaknesses: Evaluation remains largely descriptive, with limited quantitative probing of model mis-specification causes.
No inferential or sensitivity analyses (confidence intervals, statistical tests) are conducted to deepen the evaluation.
Error metrics are not benchmarked against industry standards, and uncertainty bands are omitted from visualizations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation displays generally sound research quality, clear analysis and a reasoned argument (band 4â6). While the evaluation is thoughtful, it lacks depth and inferential rigour. Awarding 6 of 12 places the work at the top of band 4â6, reflecting its substantive critical thinking balanced against its analytic omissions.

strengths: The report follows a familiar investigative structure with logical headings, tables and figures that support reader navigation.
Consistent formatting (font, spacing, page numbers, word count) and readable typesetting of equations and graphics.
Tables and figures are generally legible and placed to aid comprehension.
weaknesses: Key elements (abstract, complete subsection numbering) are missing, and some figure captions retain prompt instructions.
Citation formatting is inconsistent and some AI-sourced references are unverifiable.
Embedded data tables and interrupted text flows detract from overall polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite minor lapses (missing abstract, caption and citation issues), the structure and formatting largely support understanding, fitting the top of band 3â4. A score of 4 of 4 reflects near-complete fulfilment of presentation requirements with only modest polishing needed.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The research questionââTo what extent can Geometric Brownian Motion accurately predict the stock prices of Apple?ââis explicitly stated, expressed as an evaluative inquiry and revisited in method, results and conclusion.  It is sufficiently narrow: one firm, one model, a defined dataset and horizon.  

However, the phrasing remains somewhat broad (âto what extentâ is not operationalised into measurable success criteria until later), and there is minimal justification for the particular 2-year window and 30-day forecast horizon.  These constraints prevent the question from being fully focused or seamlessly integrated with every discussion point, keeping the strand within the 3-4 band.

Analytical work includes derivation of the GBM solution, parameter estimation, 1 000-path Monte-Carlo simulation, and calculation of multiple error metrics.  The student interprets descriptive statistics and confronts model output with actual prices, demonstrating clear, generally sound analysis.

Yet, depth is constrained: the inflated drift parameter undermines numerical validity; no sensitivity testing, confidence intervals or comparison with benchmark models (e.g. random walk) are attempted; and discussion of error metrics stops short of probing statistical significance.  These factors position the depth of analysis solidly in band 4-6 but nearer its lower end (score 4).

Specialist termsâdrift, volatility, Wiener process, MSE, RMSE, MAPE, skewness, kurtosisâare employed correctly in most cases, showing accurate grasp of mathematical and statistical vocabulary.  Equations are clearly presented and variables defined, aiding reader comprehension.

Nonetheless, a few language slips (e.g. âdecreasementsâ) and the unexplained conversion of drift to an annual figure reveal occasional imprecision.  The overall use is mostly accurate and consistent, matching band 3-4 with a score of 4.

Consistent font, spacing, page numbers, word count and table of contents are present, satisfying most layout conventions.  Mathematical notation is typeset neatly; tables and graphs are legible.

However, citation formatting is inconsistent, and inclusion of unretrievable AI chat references raises academic-presentation concerns.  Minor caption anomalies (prompt text in figure captions) also detract from overall polish.  These shortcomings align with band 3-4 (score 3).

The essay demonstrates solid knowledge of stochastic calculus: the student derives the discrete GBM formula, applies ItÃ´âs lemma and defines key parameters (drift, volatility).  Mathematical reasoning is generally correct and linked to the chosen context; simulations, descriptive statistics and error metrics are interpreted sensibly.

However, the implausibly large annualised drift (1.81 â 181 %) is neither detected nor adjusted, signalling gaps in conceptual understanding of scaling returns.  The literature base for alternative stochastic models is thin, and citations include AI chat prompts of questionable academic value.  These issues lower confidence in the studentâs command to a good rather than excellent level, aligning with band 3-4.

The studentâs title, opening paragraphs and section headings communicate the mathematical topic (Geometric Brownian Motion) and its application to Appleâs share price with reasonable clarity.  The purposeâtesting how accurately GBM predicts Appleâs stock priceâand the time horizon (30 tradingâdays, 2022-24 data) are stated early and referenced throughout, so focus is generally maintained.  

Weaknesses limiting a move to the top band include some repetitious, unfocused background material (e.g. lengthy generic comments on price prediction and investor behaviour) and occasional digressions that do not advance the mathematical investigation.  Nevertheless, the reader can follow what is being studied, why it matters and how it will be addressed, which meets the descriptor for band 3-4.

The paper follows a recognisable EE structureâtitle page, contents, introduction, method, results, discussion, conclusion and referencesâfacilitating reader navigation.  Headings, tables, equations and figures are sensibly ordered and generally support understanding.

Nevertheless, required elements such as an abstract are missing, some sub-section numbering is duplicated, and figure placement occasionally disrupts flow.  These issues prevent full marks but the overall structure remains clear, corresponding to band 3-4 (score 3).

The student selects relevant primary data (two years of Apple daily closes) and justifies GBM as a common model in mathematical finance.  The research is largely pertinent to the question, and supporting sources on stochastic calculus and financial modelling are cited, though some (e.g. AI prompts, informal websites) weaken academic rigour.  

Breadth is moderateâno alternative models or contrasting datasets are interrogatedâyet the chosen material is sufficient to sustain the investigation.  Overall quality is good rather than excellent, fitting band 4-6 with a mid-band score of 5.

The discussion links findings to the research question, acknowledging both strengths (trend tracking, low MAPE) and weaknesses (volatility underestimation, assumption limitations).  The student reflects on implications for investors versus traders and proposes GARCH as an alternative, evidencing some critical perspective.

However, evaluation is more descriptive than deeply critical: causes of model mis-specification (e.g., data window choice, drift mis-calculation) are noted but not explored quantitatively; recommendations remain general.  Argumentation is clear but not consistently sophisticated, fitting band 4-6 at a modest score of 4.

The methodology outlines data collection (Investing.com daily closes), parameter estimation (log-returns for drift and volatility) and Monte-Carlo simulation of 1 000 GBM paths, followed by statistical and error-metric comparison with actual prices.  These choices are relevant and mostly appropriate, showing some informed selection and sequencing.

Nevertheless, the range of methods is limited to one stochastic model; no alternative models, robustness checks or sensitivity analyses are implemented despite being suggested in the text.  Key assumptions (e.g. constant volatility) are acknowledged but not probed experimentally.  These omissions point to a mostly complete yet not fully comprehensive methodology, consistent with band 3-4 (score 3).

annotations.comment: The student correctly provides the word count at the start of the document, meeting the IB EE formatting requirement and enhancing transparency of the reportâs length.

This background paragraph contains lengthy generic commentary on investor behaviour and market factors that does not directly advance the investigation; consider condensing to maintain focus on GBM context.
The research question is explicitly stated as an evaluative inquiry, providing clear focus and guiding the methodology and discussion effectively.
The notation $$dW(t)\sim N(0,\sqrt{dt})$$ is misleading; the increment has variance \(dt\), so implementation should sample \(Z\sim N(0,1)\) and scale by \(\sqrt{dt}\).
The figure caption retains prompt instructions (âGenerate the graphâ), detracting from professionalism; remove these remnants and provide a concise caption.
Table 1 effectively summarizes statistics but omits inferential tests (e.g. significance of mean differences); consider adding such analysis to deepen evaluation.
The weaknesses section is cut off mid-sentence, disrupting coherence; review and complete the entry for a polished presentation.
The discussion of price prediction for customer engagement is tangential to the mathematical model; remove or refocus this content to strengthen the relevance of the introduction.
The annualized drift of 1.81 (~181%) is implausibly high, indicating a scaling error; verify calculation by adjusting for trading days and consider annualizing correctly.
The detailed overview of Appleâs listing venues adds little value to the GBM analysis; focus discussion on data characteristics affecting model assumptions.
Embedding a data table within Figure 2 hampers readability; separate the numerical results into a distinct table and streamline the figure to the plot only.
The strengths list concisely links long-term trend accuracy and MAPE performance, demonstrating clear critical evaluation of GBMâs capabilities.
The in-text citation â(Mulc 1)â is formatted inconsistently and lacks authorâdate clarity; ensure all references follow a consistent academic style.
The non-standard term âdecreasementsâ appears in the assumptions list; use established terminology (e.g. âincrementsâ) for precision and clarity.
Differentiation of investor vs. trader implications is insightful but lacks quantification; strengthen by specifying risk thresholds or decision criteria.
The methodology section outlines key steps but omits sensitivity analyses or alternative model comparisons; include robustness checks to bolster the studyâs rigor.
The 30-day simulation horizon is introduced without justification; clarify why this period was chosen and how it impacts model validity.
The visualization plan omits confidence intervals or error bands; integrating these would provide clearer insight into model uncertainty.
Interpretation of skewness and kurtosis lacks linkage to forecasting performance; discuss how distribution shape influences model accuracy.
The histogram caption still includes the prompt text (âGenerate the histogramâ); remove instructions and refine to a standard academic caption.
The conclusion reiterates prior discussion without offering new synthesis or future directions; refocus this section on key takeaways and proposed next steps.
Citations of ChatGPT prompts lack academic validity; replace or supplement with peer-reviewed sources to strengthen the scholarly foundation.
Descriptive statistics are defined here but the computed values are not presented until later, disrupting flow; consider presenting results immediately after definitions.
The statement of MAPE being âgoodâ lacks benchmarking; include industry or literature standards for error metrics to contextualize performance.
Discussion of cumulative error notes increasing divergence but remains descriptive; quantify the error growth rate or slope to strengthen analytical depth.
